megapixel-visualizer.beginner: Megapixels count the tiny dots your sensor captures — more megapixels means higher resolution, but how that translates to print sharpness, screen size, and file size depends on several factors you can explore here. Most photos viewed on screens need far fewer megapixels than you'd expect; large prints depend on viewing distance, not just DPI.

0.heading: From pixels to prints
0.text: Total pixels equals megapixels × 1,000,000. Aspect ratio determines how those pixels are split into width and height. Print size in inches equals pixels divided by DPI. A 24 MP photo at 3:2 gives 6000×4000 pixels; at 300 DPI that prints at 20×13 inches.

1.heading: The DPI myth and viewing distance
1.text: The human eye resolves about 1 arcminute of angular detail, which translates to approximately 300 PPI at 12 inches, 100 PPI at 36 inches, and 50 PPI at 72 inches. A billboard printed at 30 DPI looks perfectly sharp from across the street — the viewer's distance is doing the work.

2.heading: Megapixels vs file size
2.text: Uncompressed 16-bit TIFF is roughly 6 bytes per pixel. 14-bit compressed RAW is about 1.75 bytes per pixel. JPEG at quality 90 is around 0.3 bytes per pixel. That's why a 24 MP RAW file is 40-50 MB while the JPEG version is 7-10 MB.

3.heading: Phones and pixel binning
3.text: Modern phones advertise 48, 50, 108, or even 200 MP sensors, but output 12 MP photos by default via 4-in-1 or 9-in-1 pixel binning. Binning improves low-light performance by effectively creating larger virtual pixels. Full-resolution modes expose the advertised count at the cost of more noise in dim light.

0.label: Pixel dimensions
0.description: Megapixels × aspect ratio → width and height in pixels

1.label: Print DPI
1.description: Density of pixels per inch; determines print size and apparent sharpness

2.label: Viewing distance
2.description: Human eye acuity means prints viewed far away need much lower DPI

3.label: Pixel binning
3.description: Phones advertise higher MP but output fewer via 4-in-1 or 9-in-1 binning

4.label: Sensor area
4.description: At the same MP count, larger sensors have larger pixels and better low-light performance — see the sensor-size-comparison tool

0.text: Most web photos are viewed at 2000px or smaller — 12 MP is plenty for screens and Instagram.

1.text: For wall prints at 6-foot viewing distance, the eye only needs ~60 PPI — a 24 MP photo covers a 60-inch wide print.

2.text: Your 48 MP phone photo is typically a 12 MP binned output — only the 'full resolution' mode delivers the advertised count.

Megapixels.definition: One megapixel equals 1,000,000 pixels. It counts the total pixels your sensor captures, not the sharpness of the image.

Aspect Ratio.definition: The ratio of width to height. 3:2 is traditional for DSLRs, 4:3 for Micro Four Thirds and phones, 16:9 for video.

DPI.definition: Dots per inch — the density of pixels when printed. Higher DPI gives smaller but sharper prints from the same pixel count.

Units.definition: Switch between metric (mm/cm) and imperial (inches). Defaults to your locale.

Viewing Distance.definition: How far the viewer stands from the print. The farther away, the lower the required DPI for perceived sharpness.

Print Size.definition: The physical dimensions of the printed image at a given DPI. Calculated from pixel dimensions divided by DPI.

Bit Depth.definition: The number of bits per color channel. 8-bit (JPEG) is standard; 14-bit or 16-bit (RAW/TIFF) preserve more tonal range.

Crop Reach.definition: How many megapixels remain after cropping to a smaller sensor area. A 45 MP full-frame image cropped to APS-C is about 20 MP.

0.scenario: A 24 MP camera shoots in 3:2. What are the approximate pixel dimensions?
0.hint: Total pixels = MP × 1,000,000. Split by aspect ratio.
0.failureMessage: 24 MP means 24 million pixels. At 3:2, that's 6000 × 4000.

1.scenario: You want to print a 24 MP photo at 300 DPI in a 3:2 aspect ratio. What's the approximate print size?
1.hint: Print size in inches = pixels ÷ DPI.
1.failureMessage: At 300 DPI, a 24 MP (6000×4000) photo prints at ~20×13 inches.

2.scenario: A phone advertises 48 MP. What's the typical output resolution after pixel binning?
2.hint: Pixel binning combines 4 sensor pixels into 1 output pixel.
2.failureMessage: 4-in-1 pixel binning reduces 48 MP advertised to 12 MP output.

3.scenario: At 6-foot viewing distance, what's the minimum PPI for a print to appear sharp to the human eye?
3.hint: The eye resolves ~1 arcmin at any distance; at 72 inches that maps to ~50-60 PPI.
3.successMessage: Right! At 6 feet the eye only needs ~60 PPI for perceived sharpness.
3.failureMessage: At 6 feet the eye only resolves about 60 PPI. That's why billboards can print at very low DPI.

4.scenario: You crop a 45 MP full-frame image down to APS-C (1.5× crop factor). What's the effective megapixel count?
4.hint: Area crop factor = linear crop factor squared.
4.failureMessage: Cropping by 1.5× linearly means 2.25× area reduction. 45 ÷ 2.25 = 20 MP.
